title: United States
description: A collection of RAND research on the topic of United States
text:
Breaking Down the Federal Budget: New Tools from RAND
Economist Jeffrey Wenger explains how the RAND Budget Model works, ways it can be used to gain new insights into the federal budget, and why it matters to all Americans.
Understanding China’s National Security Decisionmaking: A Primer for U.S. Policymakers
The authors provide a framework for describing the foundational elements that shape how governments make national security decisions—information, analysis, and authorities—and apply that framework to the People’s Republic of China.
Sun-sational Incentives: Residential Solar in the Golden State
This analysis of the impact of income on the effectiveness of solar incentives in California suggests that incentives yield greater installation rates and capacity in higher-income areas than in less affluent ones—a key insight for incentive policies.
Next Generation Community Schools in New York City: An Implementation Study of Academic Enhancements in Community Schools
The authors examine the design, implementation, and outcomes of a pilot program that added academic enhancements to 20 newly established community schools in New York City.
Racial and Ethnic Differences in Quitting When Marijuana Retail Markets Are Legal: Evidence from California
This research examines whether there is a corresponding difference in the response to the introduction of legal protection of marijuana retail markets.
State-Level Summaries of Veteran Needs and Well-Being
The authors of this report present state-level estimates related to veterans’ health, well-being, and economic conditions. These estimates are presented to help inform planning, program development, and resource allocations for veterans.
'We Just Want to See People Like Us': Lessons Learned on Engaging Youth Advisory Groups for Public Health Awareness Efforts
This study evaluates a statewide youth and young adult council established as part of a public health initiative addressing adverse childhood experiences.
Patient Comments About Telehealth and In-Person Care at a Federally Qualified Health Center From Patient Experience Surveys Differ in Length, Valence, and Actionability
While telehealth visits are generally well-received, patient comments about telehealth provide less detail and contain less actionable information compared to comments about in-person visits.
How Israel’s War in Gaza (Partially) Rehabilitated Counterinsurgency Theory
Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023, terrorist attack on Israel—and the ensuing war in Gaza—have prompted a renewed reckoning with population-centric counterinsurgency strategy. If such conflicts are inevitable, then what is the “least bad” way to fight them?
NDSU, RAND Launch Research Partnership Focusing on Issues Faced in North Dakota's Rural, Remote, and Indigenous Communities
To strengthen policymaking that supports resilient, future-ready rural communities, North Dakota State University (NDSU) and RAND have launched a new research partnership focused on workforce development and other critical issues facing North Dakota.
New START’s Expiration Will Make the World Less Safe—Even if It Doesn’t Spark Another Nuclear Arms Race
The expiration of the New START treaty won't trigger an immediate arms race. But without the norms, transparency, and dialogues that have helped manage nuclear competition for decades, the strategic environment will be more dangerous for everyone.
